stem: Consider the separable equation \( \frac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]xy \). Find its general solution, then use the condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) to determine the value of \( y \) at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separating variables, \( \frac{dy}{y} = [[k]]x\,dx \), so \( \ln|y| = \frac{[[k]]x^{2}}{2} + C \) and the general solution is \( y = C_1 e^{[[k]]x^{2}/2} \). Applying \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C_1 = [[y0]] \), so \( y(x) = [[y0]]\,e^{[[k]]x^{2}/2} \). Evaluating at \( x = [[t0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( \tfrac12 \) obtained when integrating \( [[k]]x \).
This treats the equation as \( y' = [[k]]y \), ignoring the factor of \( x \).
This forgets that the constant \( C_1 \) equals the initial value \( [[y0]] \).
This flips the sign of the exponent.
This multiplies the data together instead of exponentiating.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Consider the separable equation \( \frac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]y^{2} \). Find its general solution, then use the condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) to determine the value of \( y \) at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separating variables, \( \frac{dy}{y^{2}} = [[k]]\,dx \), so \( -\frac{1}{y} = [[k]]x + C \). Applying \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C = -\dfrac{1}{[[y0]]} \), so \( y(x) = \dfrac{[[y0]]}{1 - [[k]][[y0]]x} \). At \( x = [[t0]] \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign inside the denominator.
This inverts the rational solution, multiplying instead of dividing.
This drops the initial value entirely, keeping only the growth term.
This linearizes the solution instead of using the correct rational form.
This forgets to multiply \( k \) by \( y_0 \) inside the denominator.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: Consider the separable equation \( y\,\frac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]x \). Find its general solution, then use the condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) (with \( y > 0 \)) to determine the value of \( y \) at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separating variables, \( y\,dy = [[k]]x\,dx \), so \( \frac{y^{2}}{2} = \frac{[[k]]x^{2}}{2} + C \), i.e. \( y^{2} = [[k]]x^{2} + C_1 \). Applying \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C_1 = [[y0]]^{2} \), so \( y(x) = \sqrt{[[y0]]^{2} + [[k]]x^{2}} \) (taking the positive root). At \( x = [[t0]] \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of \( k \) inside the radical.
This forgets to square \( x \) inside the constant term, giving a linear approximation.
This forgets to square \( y_0 \) before adding under the radical.
This forgets to take the square root of the implicit relation.
This drops the initial condition entirely, keeping only the \( x \)-dependent term.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The separable equation \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{x}{y} \) has general (implicit) solution \( x^{2} + y^{2} = C \). What is \( C \) for the solution curve passing through \( (x, y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This subtracts \( y^{2} \) instead of adding it.
This expands \( (x+y)^{2} \) rather than \( x^{2}+y^{2} \).
This doubles the correct constant.
This multiplies the coordinates instead of squaring and summing them.
This uses absolute values instead of squares.
This is one more than the correct constant.
This is one less than the correct constant.
